name: Algiers
summary: Algiers is moderately safe for tourists with proper precautions, though solo female travelers face significant challenges. The city has ongoing terrorism concerns and notable issues with street harassment and petty crime. Infrastructure and security presence have improved significantly in recent years.
quickVerdict: Algiers offers a fascinating blend of culture and history but requires careful planning and awareness. The US State Department maintains a Level 2 advisory due to terrorism and kidnapping risks, though most tourists experience no serious incidents when staying in main city areas with reputable guides. Street harassment is pervasive, making solo female travel particularly challenging. The capital has strong security presence and improving infrastructure, but visitors should avoid border regions and remote areas entirely.

neighborhoods.name: Hydra
neighborhoods.description: Upscale diplomatic district with excellent security, high-end hotels, and affluent residential areas. Well-patrolled with modern infrastructure and international presence.

neighborhoods.name: El Biar
neighborhoods.description: Elevated suburb with government buildings and embassies. Good security presence and relatively affluent area popular with expats and diplomats.

neighborhoods.name: Sidi Yahia
neighborhoods.description: Modern residential area in the heights of Algiers with good infrastructure and lower crime rates. Popular with middle and upper-class residents.

neighborhoods.name: City Center
neighborhoods.description: Main commercial and business district with heavy police presence but also higher crime rates. Pickpocketing and harassment common in crowded areas.

neighborhoods.name: The Casbah
neighborhoods.description: Historic UNESCO site that's fascinating during the day with guides but can be unsafe after dark. Narrow alleys and limited police access create security concerns at night.

neighborhoods.name: Bab El Oued
neighborhoods.description: Densely populated working-class neighborhood with higher crime rates and social tensions. Limited tourist infrastructure and frequent security incidents.

scams.name: Fake Tour Guides
scams.description: Men pose as licensed guides in tourist areas, especially around the Casbah, then demand excessive payment or steal from tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Only use guides arranged through reputable hotels or tour companies. Verify official credentials and agree on prices upfront.

scams.name: Romance Scams
scams.description: Online scammers target foreign women through dating sites, eventually requesting money or attempting to lure victims to Algeria for forced marriage.
scams.howToAvoid: Be extremely cautious about online relationships. Never send money or travel to meet someone you've only known online.

scams.name: Overpriced Taxi Rides
scams.description: Taxi drivers quote inflated prices to tourists or take unnecessarily long routes, especially from the airport.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ride-hailing apps like Yassir, agree on fares before starting, or ask your hotel to arrange transportation.

scams.name: Camera Drop Scam
scams.description: Locals ask tourists to take their photo, then deliberately drop the camera and demand compensation for the 'broken' device.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely decline to take photos for strangers, or if you must, be very careful with their equipment and photograph any existing damage first.

soloFemale.overview: Solo female travel in Algiers is challenging due to pervasive street harassment and cultural attitudes toward unaccompanied women. While not impossible for experienced travelers, it requires significant precautions and cultural sensitivity. Many local women report receiving dozens of comments daily, and foreign women attract even more attention. The conservative Muslim culture means women are expected to dress modestly and avoid certain behaviors.
tips: Always dress conservatively covering shoulders, arms, and legs, and consider wearing a headscarf in some areas
Never walk alone after dark and stick to busy, well-lit areas during the day
Arrange transportation through hotels rather than flagging taxis on the street, especially after sunset
Consider hiring a male guide for sightseeing, as harassment decreases significantly when accompanied by men
Book accommodation in upscale areas like Hydra or Sidi Yahia where women have more freedom

nightSafety.overview: Algiers becomes significantly less safe after dark, with most locals avoiding unnecessary outdoor activities. The city has limited nightlife options mainly confined to hotel bars and restaurants. Muggings and petty crime increase substantially after sunset, and women face additional harassment risks. Even experienced travelers should exercise extreme caution when venturing out at night.
tips: Avoid walking anywhere alone after dark, even in seemingly safe neighborhoods
Use hotel-arranged transportation or reputable taxi services like Yassir rather than walking or flagging street taxis
Stick to hotel bars and restaurants for evening entertainment rather than exploring the city
If you must go out, travel in groups and inform hotel staff of your plans and expected return
Keep valuables secure and carry minimal cash, as armed robberies can occur in quiet areas

transport.metro: Algiers has North Africa's first metro system which is modern, clean, and generally safe during daytime hours. The system operates from 5am to 11pm with trains every 5 minutes. A single ticket costs 50 dinars with day passes available for 150 dinars.
transport.rideshare: Yassir and Heetch are the dominant ride-hailing apps, offering fixed pricing and good safety for tourists. Both apps require cash payment despite booking through the app. These services are much safer than street taxis and eliminate price negotiation.
transport.taxis: Official taxis are widely available but often lack working meters. Many drivers overcharge tourists significantly. Hotel-arranged taxis are much safer and more reliable, though more expensive. Shared taxis called 'louages' operate on fixed routes but can be crowded.
transport.tips: Use the metro during daylight hours and ride-hailing apps for other times. Avoid public buses due to harassment and theft risks. Never use unofficial transport, especially after dark.

customs: Dress modestly, covering shoulders and legs especially when visiting mosques or religious sites
Remove shoes when entering homes and some traditional establishments
Use your right hand for eating, greeting, and giving gifts as the left hand is considered unclean
Greet elders first when entering a room and show respect to older individuals
Avoid public displays of affection as they are not culturally acceptable
During Ramadan, be respectful of those fasting and avoid eating or drinking in public during daylight hours

health.overview: Algeria offers free universal healthcare for citizens, but the quality varies significantly. Private healthcare is much better but expensive. Major hospitals in Algiers meet basic international standards though may lack some specialized equipment. Medical evacuation insurance is strongly recommended.
health.water: Tap water is not consistently safe to drink, especially outside major cities. Stick to sealed bottled water and avoid ice. Be cautious with raw vegetables and salads unless you know how they were washed.
health.vaccinations: Routine vaccinations should be up to date including MMR, hepatitis A and B. Some travelers may need typhoid, meningitis, or rabies vaccines. Consult a travel medicine clinic at least 4 weeks before departure.
health.altitude: Algiers is at sea level. The climate is Mediterranean with hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters. Air quality can be poor in urban areas due to traffic and dust.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Algiers: 5 Chemin Cheikh Bachir Ibrahimi, El-Biar 16030 Algiers. Emergency contact: +213 770 08 2000

faq.a: Algiers is moderately safe for tourists who take proper precautions. Stay in main tourist areas, use reputable guides, avoid remote regions, and be aware of your surroundings. The government has invested heavily in tourism security.

Algiers is not safe for tourists at night. Most locals avoid unnecessary outdoor activities after dark. Stick to hotel bars and restaurants, use arranged transportation, and never walk alone after sunset.

faq.q: Is Algiers safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Solo female travel in Algiers is challenging due to pervasive street harassment and cultural attitudes. While possible for experienced travelers with significant precautions, consider traveling with companions or joining organized tours.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Algiers?
faq.a: Avoid Bab El Oued neighborhood, the Casbah after dark, all border regions, and remote areas outside the city. Stay in upscale districts like Hydra, El Biar, and the main city center during daylight hours.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Algiers?
faq.a: The metro is safe during daytime hours and tramways are generally reliable. Avoid buses due to harassment and theft risks. Use ride-hailing apps like Yassir or hotel-arranged taxis for other transport needs.
